Understanding the original context — a sign given in darkness, pointing to a presence that will not abandon — makes the incarnation more profound, not less.

questions.question: How did Ahaz and Isaiah respond differently to the Lord and his word? What does this teach us about the nature of faith and the temptation to reject the Lord? (Look again at 8:12–15 and its NT applications.) What does it look like for us to put our faith in the Lord like Isaiah?
questions.answer: Ahaz faced the same Lord and the same crisis as Isaiah — but chose to manage the situation in his own way. Isaiah chose to fear the Lord rather than the Assyrians. The NT applications of 8:14 (Romans 9:33; 1 Peter 2:8) show the Lord remains a stone of stumbling or cornerstone depending on how one responds. Faith like Isaiah’s: taking the Lord’s word seriously, refusing security in what the world offers, willing to stand out from the crowd.

questions.question: Spend time reflecting on 9:1–7. What has the coming of Immanuel achieved? What difference does Jesus make? We don’t yet see all of this in completion, so what difference do these verses make for how we live now?
questions.answer: Open reflection question. The government is on his shoulders — not ours. The light has come; darkness does not have the last word. The joy is not yet complete but it is real and coming. The titles of v6 tell us who bears the weight of history. We live in the tension between the dawning of the light and its full brightness.
questions.leaderNote: Allow time for this final question — opportunity for genuine worship, not just intellectual discussion. The titles of 9:6 are worth dwelling on one at a time. Don’t rush to the end of the session here.

chapters.title: Isaiah 9:8–10:34 — Assyria is Only the Lord’s Tool
chapters.bigIdea: Assyria is only the Lord’s tool and will one day come under his judgment, so submit to the Lord’s discipline and return to him.

background.body:
This session covers 9:8–10:34 and should be read alongside chapter 11, which is studied in the following session. The notes suggest reading all the way to the end of chapter 11 before the session so the Messianic promise of 11:1–16 is in view even as the group works through the darkness of 9:8–10:34.

The fourfold refrain (9:8–10:4). The section is structured around a repeated refrain: ‘Yet for all this, his anger is not turned away, his hand is still upraised.’ This divides the passage into four distinct indictments, each describing a different aspect of Israel’s sin and the corresponding judgment. The cumulative effect is important — Israel’s sin is stubborn and God’s response is patient but unrelenting.

The Assyrian paradox (10:5–19). One of the most theologically sophisticated passages in Isaiah. The Assyrians are simultaneously the Lord’s instrument of judgment and themselves guilty of pride and overreach. The Lord has ‘sent’ them (v6) but the Assyrians’ intentions are entirely their own (v7) — they are not aware they are serving his purposes. The axe and woodsman metaphor (v15) makes the point starkly. This paradox — human agency fully exercised within divine sovereignty — is one of the deepest theological threads in Isaiah and Scripture as a whole.

The remnant returns (10:20–34). Shear-Jashub (‘a remnant will return’ — the name of Isaiah’s son in 7:3) is explicitly echoed in 10:21–22. The promise has been hanging in the air since chapter 7 and here it is fulfilled, at least in promise: there will be survivors who truly trust in the Lord.

Midian and Egypt (10:26). The references to Gideon’s victory over Midian (Judges 7) and the deliverance from Egypt (Exodus 14) are typological, not nostalgic. Both were victories against overwhelming odds, entirely the Lord’s doing. The Lord has done this before and can do it again: Assyria may seem unstoppable, but the Lord is King.

keyPoints.body:
The fourfold refrain creates a cumulative sense of both sin and patience. ‘His hand is still upraised’ — the Lord has not yet struck, but judgment is coming. Each section shows Israel digging deeper into rebellion while the Lord’s discipline fails to provoke repentance.

The Lord uses pagan powers to discipline his people — but he does not endorse them. The Assyrians do what they want — conquering, plundering, building empire. The Lord is using what they do for his purposes. Their pride, which leads them to think they operate independently of any god, is precisely what will bring them under judgment. The axe cannot claim to be greater than the woodsman.

Pride is the common thread between Israel and Assyria. Israel’s sin in 9:8–10:4 is repeatedly characterised by arrogance — ‘We will rebuild with dressed stone’ (9:10): self-confident recovery rather than repentance. The Assyrians’ sin (10:7–14) is the same: ‘By the strength of my hand I have done this’ (10:13). Both are claiming for themselves what belongs to God alone.

The remnant promise is alive even in the darkest passages. Shear-Jashub has been hanging in the air since 7:3. Now in 10:20–22 it is fulfilled in promise: a remnant will return. They will stop relying on human powers and truly trust in the Lord.

Why chapter 11 matters after 9:8–10:34. The darkness is real and sustained. Chapter 11 is the answer. Don’t end the session without pointing ahead — the stump of 6:13 and the cutting down of 10:33–34 are the necessary prelude to the shoot of 11:1.

exegetical.body:
9:10: ‘We will rebuild with dressed stone.’ The response to discipline is not repentance but defiant self-improvement. The means of their false security (allies, enemies) become the instruments of judgment.

9:13–17: Unrepentant leadership. ‘The people have not returned to him who struck them’ (v13). The leaders are specifically indicted for misleading the people. Poor leadership is both judgment on the people and itself a sin.

9:18–21: Wickedness consuming itself. The self-consuming nature of sin is graphically described: they turn on each other and on Judah. Sin does not simply damage others — it ultimately destroys the sinner.

10:1–4: Injustice and the reversal of roles. Those who made others vulnerable will themselves become vulnerable. The question of v3 — ‘Where will you flee for help?’ — is devastating.

10:5: ‘Woe to the Assyrian, the rod of my anger.’ A startling statement. The Lord himself calls Assyria his ‘rod’ and ‘club’. This is not an endorsement of Assyrian policy but a statement of divine sovereignty: even pagan empire-building serves the Lord’s purposes without the empire-builders knowing it.

10:15: The axe metaphor. ‘Does the axe raise itself above the person who swings it?’ One of the clearest expressions of divine sovereignty and human accountability held together in Isaiah. The Assyrians think they are agents of history; they are the tools. The Lord is the woodsman.

10:26: Midian and Egypt. Gideon’s rout of the Midianites (Judges 7:19–25) and the crossing of the Red Sea (Exodus 14:15–31) were victories against overwhelming odds where only the Lord could be credited. When the Lord acts, military superiority is irrelevant.

pastorals.body:
The fourfold ‘his hand is still upraised’ can feel relentless and hopeless if the group doesn’t keep the remnant promise in view. The Lord’s anger is real, his patience is long, and the remnant will return. The sustained darkness makes the light of chapter 11 more brilliant.

The Assyrian paradox (human agency within divine sovereignty) is theologically rich but can be confusing. Don’t try to resolve the tension — it is one of the most consistent patterns in Scripture (cf. Genesis 50:20; Acts 2:23). The goal is to help the group hold both truths simultaneously: the Assyrians are fully responsible for their pride, and the Lord is using them for his purposes.

The question about seemingly unstoppable powers today is one of the most pastorally relevant in this session. The point is not to identify modern Assyrias with confidence but to trust the Lord’s sovereignty even when human power seems overwhelming.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the group getting stuck in the detail of the four indictments and losing the shape of the whole. Aim to land the main insight from each section and keep moving. Save time for the Assyrian paradox (10:5–19), which is the theological heart of the session.

questions.header: 9:8–10:4: The Lord’s hand is still upraised
questions.question: This section is in four parts, each ending with ‘Yet for all this, his anger is not turned away, his hand is still upraised.’ In groups, go through each part and discuss what Israel’s sin is, how the Lord will judge them, and how his judgment fits their sin.
questions.answer:
9:8–12: Pride and arrogance — self-confident rebuilding rather than repentance. The Lord uses their allies and enemies (in whom they trust) to destroy them.

9:13–17: Unrepentant in the face of judgment; ungodly leaders and lying prophets. The Lord takes no pleasure in them and does not pity them — all parts of society are guilty, including the leaders who should know better.

9:18–21: Wickedness so rife it is consuming everything. The Lord’s judgment is the real fire beneath it. They turn even on each other.

10:1–4: Unjust laws and oppression of the vulnerable. The Lord will make them vulnerable in turn — they will have nowhere to flee.
questions.leaderNote: Works well as a group exercise: four sub-groups, one per section, 5–7 minutes, then bring together. The key insight to draw out: each judgment fits the sin — a form of poetic justice reflecting the Lord’s character as both holy and fair.

questions.header: 10:5–19: Does the axe raise itself above the person who swings it?
questions.question: How does the Lord see Assyria? What is Assyria’s divine purpose? (vv5–6)
questions.answer: Assyria is nothing more than the Lord’s tool of discipline — a ‘rod’ and a ‘club’. He is the one dispatching the Assyrians. Their power over Israel is entirely derived from and dependent on the Lord’s commission.

questions.header: 10:5–19: Does the axe raise itself above the person who swings it?
questions.question: How are the Assyrians’ intentions different from the Lord’s? How do the Assyrians see themselves? (vv7–14) Why is their pride such a big problem? (v15)
questions.answer: The Assyrians intend to destroy many nations and make themselves great. They are hugely self-confident, pointing to their long list of conquered nations as proof (vv9–11, 13–14). But this pride is precisely the problem (v15): the axe does not get to claim credit for what the woodsman does. The tool that claims to be greater than its user has confused itself about the nature of reality.

questions.header: 10:5–19: Does the axe raise itself above the person who swings it?
questions.question: What will the Lord do? (vv12, 16–19)
questions.answer: The Lord will punish the king of Assyria for his wilful pride (v12), after finishing using them to discipline Jerusalem. He will consume them and make them weak and few (vv16–19). The reversal is pointed: they seemed all-powerful and uncountable, but the Lord will bring them low. This actually happened historically when the Assyrian army was destroyed outside Jerusalem (37:36).

questions.question: What will be the result of the Lord’s judgment on Israel?
questions.answer: Devastating destruction, but a small remnant will return to the Lord. They will stop relying on human powers — specifically Assyria, the very thing they had been running to — and truly trust in the Lord. The name Shear-Jashub (‘a remnant will return’ — Isaiah’s son in 7:3) is echoed explicitly in vv21–22. There is hope beyond judgment.

questions.question: What is the experience of God’s people with the Assyrians? (vv24, 28–34)
questions.answer: They are afraid and oppressed as the Lord uses the Assyrians to judge Israel. Verses 28–34 give a terrifying march southward toward Jerusalem, town by town — a sense of mounting dread graphically narrated.

questions.question: How does the Lord change their perspective on their situation? (vv25–27)
questions.answer: The Lord puts a limit on it. His anger against his people will be finished, and he will then punish the Assyrians. He will remove the burden of Assyrian rule. The very yoke that seemed permanent will be broken.

questions.question: What is the significance of referring to Midian and Egypt in v26?
questions.answer: Both were victories against great odds — Gideon’s rout of the Midianites (Judges 7:19–25) and the crossing of the Red Sea (Exodus 14:15–31). In both cases only the Lord could take the credit. The point: Assyria might seem unstoppable but the Lord is King, and he has done this before.

questions.question: What does today’s passage teach us about what the Lord values in his people?
questions.answer: He values repentance, humility, and genuine trust — and is grieved by stubborn self-confidence that treats his discipline as an inconvenience to manage rather than a call to return. The fourfold refrain is not a picture of a God who enjoys judgment but of a God whose patience is being tested by a people who refuse to return.

questions.question: What does today’s passage teach us about the Lord’s promises?
questions.answer: Even in the darkest judgment, the remnant promise holds. Shear-Jashub has been waiting since 7:3 and here it is fulfilled. The Lord does not abandon his purposes even when his people abandon him. His promises outlast their failures.

questions.question: What does today’s passage teach us about the Lord’s sovereignty? How does this help us think about seemingly unstoppable and scary powers today?
questions.answer: The most powerful empire of the ancient world is, in the Lord’s hands, just a rod. The Assyrians do not know they are serving his purposes; that does not change the fact that they are. This gives the people of God a radically different perspective on terrifying human powers: not denial of their danger, but confidence that they operate within the Lord’s purposes and cannot ultimately prevail against his plans.

questions.question: Why do you think chapter 11 is so significant after what we have seen in chapters 9–10?
questions.answer: Chapters 9–10 have described the cutting down of Israel, Assyria, and all proud powers. Chapter 10 ends with the Lord himself lopping off the tall trees (vv33–34). Then 11:1: ‘A shoot will come up from the stump of Jesse.’ Out of total apparent death comes new life. The Branch does not come from the impressive cedars of human power but from the humblest point possible: a stump. This is the shape of the gospel.
questions.leaderNote: If the group is reading ahead to chapter 11, use this question to preview the next session. The shoot from the stump of Jesse is the answer to everything chapters 9–10 have described.

chapters.title: Isaiah 11–12 — Praise the Lord for His Righteous Branch
chapters.bigIdea: Praise the Lord because his righteous Branch will rule in peace and gather his people home.

background.body:
Chapters 11–12 form the climax of the first major section of Isaiah (chs 6–12, the ‘Book of the King’) and function as a resolution of everything that has preceded. The darkness of chapters 9–10 finds its answer not in political success or military reversal but in the coming of the Branch from Jesse’s stump.

The Branch (11:1). The Hebrew choter (shoot) and netser (Branch) convey new life emerging from apparent death. The stump of Jesse — not of David the king but of Jesse his father, the original family before the royal line — suggests a return to the very roots, a new beginning from the most basic point. This echoes the cutting down of 6:13 and 10:33–34.

The Spirit (11:2). The seven-fold description of the Spirit is one of the richest pneumatological texts in the OT. In Jewish tradition these seven gifts are associated with the Messianic age. Isaiah 42:1 and 61:1 return to the Spirit resting on the Servant/Anointed One. The NT identifies Jesus’s baptism as the fulfilment of this anointing (Matthew 3:16–17; Luke 4:18).

The new creation (11:6–9). The vision of predator and prey at peace deliberately echoes and reverses the Fall. The passage is closely linked to Isaiah 65:17–25 and Revelation 21–22.

The second Exodus (11:10–16). The gathering of the scattered people of God from the four corners of the earth is described as a new Exodus, vastly expanded: not just Israel from Egypt but scattered exiles from Assyria, Egypt, Cush, Elam, Babylonia, Hamath, and the islands of the sea. The NT applies this to the gathering of the nations through Christ.

The song of chapter 12. Chapter 12 is a short psalm of thanksgiving — two stanzas that bookend the first major section of Isaiah. It echoes the Song of the Sea (Exodus 15) and looks ahead to the songs of the redeemed in Revelation 5:9 and 15:3. The right response to everything in chapters 1–11 is praise.

keyPoints.body:
The Branch is the opposite of Assyria in every way. Chapter 10 described Assyria as lofty, tall, arrogant, and destructive. The Branch comes from a stump, is filled with the Spirit, and judges by righteousness not appearances. Everything Assyria is, the Branch is not; everything Assyria lacks, the Branch has.

The Spirit equips the Branch for everything the leaders of Israel lacked. The catalogue of 11:2–3 reads like a systematic answer to everything wrong with Israel’s leadership in chapters 1–10: wisdom where there was folly, understanding where there was blindness, knowledge and fear of the Lord where there was pride and idolatry.

The Branch’s rule overturns the Fall. The vision of 11:6–9 is a theological statement about the scope of redemption: not just political restoration but the healing of creation itself. The root of all evil is ignorance of the Lord; the earth being filled with his knowledge (v9) addresses the root.

The gathering is for all nations, not just Israel. The nations themselves will rally to the Branch (v10). This is the fulfilment of the promise of 2:1–5 that opened this section. The study has come full circle.

The right response is praise. Chapter 12 gives the group the words. Don’t rush past it — it is the conclusion and the response, and it deserves to be read aloud.

exegetical.body:
‘Jesse’ rather than ‘David’ (11:1). By the time of the Messiah’s coming, the royal Davidic line will have been so reduced that it is back to Jesse — the original family before the glory days. The Messiah does not come from a lineage at the height of its power but from a stump. This is the theology of the cross in seed form.

‘He will not judge by what he sees with his eyes’ (11:3). A direct contrast to the leaders of Israel who have judged by appearances — favouring the rich, oppressing the poor, calling good evil. The Branch’s justice is based on truth, not social status or political expediency.

‘The wolf will live with the lamb’ (11:6). Functions on multiple levels. Literally: the reversal of natural hostility. Politically: the great predator nations and vulnerable prey will no longer relate in those terms. The categories of power and weakness will be transformed under the Branch’s rule.

‘The root of Jesse’ (11:10). Slightly different from the ‘shoot from the stump of Jesse’ in v1 — here the root itself that the nations seek. Paul quotes this in Romans 15:12 as the basis for the Gentile mission: ‘The Root of Jesse will spring up, one who will arise to rule over the nations; in him the Gentiles will hope.’

‘The wells of salvation’ (12:3). Water drawn freely from a source that does not run dry. John 4:14 (the woman at the well) and John 7:37–38 (streams of living water) both draw on this imagery. Salvation is not a scarce resource but an inexhaustible spring.

pastorals.body:
This is one of the most joyful sessions in the whole study. After the sustained darkness of chapters 9–10, the group has earned the right to dwell in chapters 11–12 with delight. Some of the best responses to passages like 11:6–9 and 12:1–6 are simply silence, reading aloud together, or quiet prayer.

The new creation imagery of 11:6–9 can raise questions about whether this is literal or figurative. It is not necessary to resolve this — the theological point is clear regardless: the Branch’s redemption extends to the whole created order. Help the group feel the breadth of that vision.

The application questions at the end ask the group to reflect on the whole of chapters 1–12. This is a good opportunity to step back and see the arc: from the devastating indictment of chapter 1 to the song of praise in chapter 12. Allow time for that bigger reflection.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the group treating chapter 12 as an epilogue rather than a conclusion. It is the right response to everything in chapters 1–11 — the answer to ‘so what do we do with all of this?’ is praise and proclamation. If time is short, make sure chapter 12 gets its own space.

questions.question: How does the Branch contrast with the way the Assyrians are described in chapter 10?
questions.answer: The Assyrians are lofty, tall, impressive trees that the Lord cuts down (10:33–34). The Branch comes from a stump: humble, apparently lifeless, the least promising origin imaginable. Everything the Assyrians claimed for themselves — power, wisdom, victory — the Branch actually possesses, but through the Spirit rather than through pride.

questions.question: What will the Branch be like? How is he different from Assyria and from the leaders of Israel and Judah?
questions.answer: From David’s line (Jesse); full of the Spirit of the Lord; wisdom and understanding; counsel and might; knowledge and fear of the Lord — delighting in the fear of the Lord; judging righteously not superficially; bringing justice to the poor; striking down the wicked; characterised by righteousness and faithfulness. Every quality is the direct opposite of what the Assyrians claimed and what Israel’s leaders demonstrated in chapters 1–10.

questions.question: How are vv1–5 fulfilled in Jesus?
questions.answer: Jesus is the Davidic Messiah, anointed with the Spirit at his baptism (Matthew 3:16–17; cf. Luke 4:18, quoting Isaiah 61:1). Everything about his ministry — his wisdom in teaching, his righteous judgment, his care for the poor, his confrontation of the wicked — can be described in the categories of these verses. Invite the group to give specific NT examples for each characteristic.

questions.question: What will the rule of the Branch be like?
questions.answer: Characterised by the global peace and harmony of creation: wolf and lamb, leopard and goat, calf and lion at peace. A little child will lead them. Nothing will harm or destroy on the Lord’s holy mountain because the earth will be filled with the knowledge of the Lord as the waters cover the sea.

questions.question: How does the Branch’s rule fit into the overall story of salvation history?
questions.answer: This is the ultimate overturning of the Fall. The enmity, pain, and death that came into creation in Genesis 3 are reversed. It points forward to Revelation 21 — the new creation where there is no more death, mourning, crying, or pain. The Branch’s reign is the healing of everything that went wrong at the beginning.

questions.question: What will the Branch do for the Lord’s people?
questions.answer: Gather them from across the earth — from Assyria, Egypt, Cush, Elam, Babylonia, and the islands of the sea — and destroy any opposition. It will be like a new and greater Exodus: the Lord will dry up the gulf of the Egyptian sea and provide a highway for the remnant.

questions.question: How does this point us forward to the ministry of Jesus?
questions.answer: Christ gathers the people of God from across all nations. Note that the Branch gathers not just scattered Israelite exiles but the nations themselves (v10 — ‘the nations will rally to him’). This points forward to the work of Jesus when people from every language, tribe, and nation will be gathered to him, and nothing will be able to stop him building his church.
questions.leaderNote: Paul quotes v10 in Romans 15:12 to justify his Gentile mission. It is worth pointing the group to this — it shows that the universal scope of the Branch’s gathering is not a NT invention but the fulfilment of what Isaiah promised.

questions.header: 12:1–6: The song of the people of God
questions.question: How are the people of God to respond to all this?
questions.answer: With praise and proclamation. The song of chapter 12 is both a response to what the Lord has done and a commission to tell others about it. ‘Give praise to the Lord, proclaim his name; make known among the nations what he has done’ (v4).

questions.header: 12:1–6: The song of the people of God
questions.question: What is to be the focus of their praise and proclamation?
questions.answer: The salvation of the Lord. His anger turned away. His protection. The joyful proclamation of glorious things he has done for his people — not as private spiritual experience but as news to be made known among the nations. The image of drawing water from the wells of salvation (v3) speaks of abundance freely received: salvation is not scarce but an inexhaustible source.

questions.question: This ends the first main section in Isaiah. How has what we have learned help us understand the ministry of Jesus better? How does it help us understand the story of salvation history?
questions.answer: Open reflection question — invite the group to name what has struck them most from the first six sessions. The arc from chapter 1 (devastating indictment) through the Immanuel promise (ch7), the darkness of chapters 9–10, to the Branch and the song of chapter 12 is itself a miniature of the whole gospel story: diagnosis, promise, darkness, fulfilment, praise.
questions.leaderNote: A good moment to step back from detailed exegesis and invite broader reflection. What has changed in how the group reads the OT? What understanding of Jesus has deepened? What have they learned about the character of God? Allow at least 5–7 minutes.

questions.question: How can we now look differently at our world? What difference does it make to see what the Branch will do ‘on that day’?
questions.answer: Knowing that the Branch’s rule will ultimately cover the earth as the waters cover the sea gives the people of God a radically different perspective on the darkness and brokenness of the present. We live between the first coming of the Branch and his final coming. That in-between position shapes everything about how we face the present.

questions.question: How can we respond in praise and proclamation to the Lord?
questions.answer: Open application question. The pattern of chapter 12: receive the Lord’s salvation → respond in praise → proclaim to others. Help the group think concretely about what ‘making known among the nations what he has done’ looks like in their specific context.
questions.leaderNote: Consider closing the session by reading chapter 12 aloud together — perhaps alternating verses or reading responsively. After six sessions of sustained engagement with judgment and hope, the song of chapter 12 deserves to be heard as a song, not just studied as a text.

She has worked as a Latin and history teacher, taught English in Asia and lived in East Africa. She is married to David, with whom she has four daughters. They divide their time between Scotland and West Africa.
